video_title: When Passive Money Meets Mega IPOs | Protect the Pile Episode 13
gist: Hedgeye's Protect the Pile panel (RPK, Sam, David Salem, ETF/index specialist Brooks Cartwright) dissects how S&P/index inclusion rules may bend to admit mega-cap IPOs like SpaceX, why liquidity is driving all-time-high equities, the AI capex buildout's bottlenecks and weak-zebra risks, and a looming oil supply shock from a closed Strait of Hormuz.

themes.theme: Liquidity as the dominant market driver

insights.claim: Global dollar liquidity is running ~78% annualized growth, with US M3 at 15% one-month annualized and M2 at 10%, which the panel frames as the primary force pinning the S&P 500 at all-time highs near 7590.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kent (RPK)
insights.so_what: If liquidity is the swing factor, equity bulls should monitor M3/M2 rates of change rather than fundamentals; deceleration below nominal GDP is the warning signal.
insights.quote: I have borrowed that... and refashioned it as it's the liquidity stupid.

insights.claim: RPK uses a rebuilt M3 proxy (bank deposits, money market funds, currency, a Eurodollar estimate) with a rule of thumb: liquidity growth above nominal GDP is 'green,' around/below is 'yellow,' and sub-zero is contraction that typically precedes a problem.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kent (RPK)
insights.so_what: A reusable liquidity dashboard for timing risk-on/risk-off; a sustained move toward zero would flag asset-price contraction risk.

insights.claim: A strengthening dollar is a global liquidity drag while a weakening dollar increases purchasing power and buying capacity for ex-US assets.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kent (RPK)
insights.so_what: Dollar direction is a top-down liquidity input; watch DXY as a leading variable for global risk appetite.

themes.theme: Index inclusion mechanics and mega-cap IPOs

insights.claim: S&P is running a referendum on whether to suspend its 10% minimum free-float requirement to admit a wave of giant IPOs that may float only ~8% of shares, while keeping float-weighting intact.
insights.speaker: Brooks Cartwright
insights.so_what: Rule changes determine which mega-IPOs passive funds must buy; a referendum outcome is a tradable catalyst for IPO positioning.
insights.quote: what kind of shape can we contort our rulebook into

insights.claim: The big four index providers collectively force-buy roughly 19% of a stock's free float once eligible (S&P ~13%, Russell ~3.5-4%, MSCI ~2-3%, Vanguard similar).
insights.speaker: Brooks Cartwright
insights.so_what: Quantifies the mechanical passive bid on newly included names; the smaller the real float, the larger the percentage of float passive must absorb.

insights.claim: A $2 trillion SpaceX selling only 10% would have ~$200 billion free float, placing it around the 75th-80th position in the S&P, not the top 10 as many assume, with weight rising over years as insiders sell.
insights.speaker: Brooks Cartwright
insights.so_what: Corrects a popular misconception; the passive-buying impact of mega-IPOs is back-loaded and gradual, not an instant top-10 inclusion event.
insights.quote: people that think that that that SpaceX would go public and it would all of a sudden end up in the top top 10 holdings in spy just like mistaken

insights.claim: S&P revoked its dual-share-class exclusion rule around 2020-2023 after recognizing 'the horse was out of the barn,' since too many tech founders had adopted founder-control structures like Zuckerberg's.
insights.speaker: Brooks Cartwright
insights.so_what: Founder-control share structures are now permanently embedded in major indices; governance discounts no longer bar inclusion.

insights.claim: On a true (non-free-float-adjusted) basis the US public market is even more concentrated in the mega-caps than headline S&P weights suggest, because founders hold huge insider stakes that float-adjustment removes.
insights.speaker: David Salem
insights.so_what: Underlying economic concentration exceeds index optics; a reason concentration risk is understated by standard weight analysis.

insights.claim: A Murray Stahl/Horizon Kinetics study (extended by Bessembinder) found essentially 100% of long-term public-stock gains come from founder-run companies during the founders' tenure, with gains stopping once founders leave.
insights.speaker: David Salem
insights.so_what: Supports an investment tilt toward founder-led firms (SpaceX, Meta, Roblox), though RPK flags survivorship and tech-skew caveats.

themes.theme: Passive vs active ownership and behavioral signals

insights.claim: At a standing-room Bernstein conference SanDisk session, fewer than 10 of ~120 attendees raised hands as owners despite the stock being up several thousand percent, which David Salem attributes largely to SanDisk's tiny ~15bp S&P weight not obligating active managers to own it.
insights.speaker: Sam / David Salem
insights.so_what: Crowded-looking AI memory winners may be under-owned by active managers; index weight, not conviction, dictates obligatory ownership (Nvidia at ~7% must be owned).

insights.claim: Today's market behaves very differently from 1999: investors look at parabolic charts (SanDisk, Micron, Intel, AMD) and admit they don't understand them and stay underexposed, rather than piling in euphorically.
insights.speaker: Sam
insights.so_what: Skepticism among professionals suggests the AI/memory rally lacks classic blow-off euphoria, arguing against an imminent 1999-style top.

themes.theme: AI capex buildout: bottlenecks and weak zebras

insights.claim: GE Vernova's CEO cited spectacular turbine demand but flagged that data-center projects are being delayed by component shortages and local political pushback requiring behind-the-meter power strategies, stretching the capex boom out by extra years.
insights.speaker: Sam
insights.so_what: Delays convert a sharp capex spike into a longer-duration boom ('less pig-in-the-python'); marginally negative near-term but extends the cycle for suppliers.
insights.quote: instead of the big pig and the python it might actually make it a little bit more stretched out

insights.claim: TSMC refuses to grow foundry capex beyond ~12-50% even though Jensen/Nvidia wants 30-50%, making TSMC the tail that wags the dog and a hard constraint on AI compute supply.
insights.speaker: Sam
insights.so_what: Supply growth is gated by TSMC's conservatism, supporting tight chip availability and pricing power for memory/foundry names.

insights.claim: Meta raised its capex guidance entirely on price (not volume), and Zuckerberg already has a plan B to outsource compute like a cloud provider if it overbuilds.
insights.speaker: RPK / Sam
insights.so_what: Price-driven capex increases signal compute scarcity and rising input costs; a key watch item is whether any hyperscaler 'blinks' and partners rather than escalating.

insights.claim: Data-center capacity is so tight that Anthropic is now leasing space from xAI, and the panel notes Oracle has effectively bet the farm on being the marginal excess-capacity provider, with Oracle's stock reviving as a slowdown signals it won't 'blow itself up' as fast.
insights.speaker: RPK / Brooks
insights.so_what: Compute leasing among rivals confirms acute scarcity; Oracle CDS/equity is a barometer of capex-debt stress.

insights.claim: Oracle is still facing roughly negative $25-30 billion of free cash flow estimates over the next two years, keeping its buildout bet dependent on a future payoff.
insights.speaker: Sam
insights.so_what: Quantifies Oracle's cash burn risk; a metric to track for whether the AI-capacity bet pays off or strains the balance sheet.

insights.claim: If AI capex growth (the forecast ~$600B-$1T) merely slows rather than collapses, the weak zebras are the obvious capex beneficiaries across tech, industrials, and energy whose orders slip from 2026 into 2027.
insights.speaker: RPK / Sam
insights.so_what: A deceleration (not cancellation) reprices second-derivative capex names; positioning should distinguish order-backlog timing rather than blanket-selling the supply chain.

themes.theme: Oil supply shock from a closed Strait of Hormuz

insights.claim: US crude inventories drew 13 million barrels this week (30 million over two weeks), and the panel argues a closed Strait of Hormuz cannot be resolved with a switch-flip even if a ceasefire is signed, because ships must sail and damaged infrastructure rebuilt.
insights.speaker: RPK / David Salem
insights.so_what: Inventory draws plus a slow reopening point to a looming physical oil shortage; bullish physical oil even as paper prices stay 'shockingly low.'

insights.claim: David Salem argues the paper oil market is significantly 'offsides' versus the physical market, and that the shockingly low futures price implies the market expects demand destruction (starting with jet fuel) to balance a looming physical shortage.
insights.speaker: David Salem
insights.so_what: Falling oil prices may signal expected demand destruction/refinery turnarounds rather than supply relief; a contrarian read of the curve.
insights.quote: what the market is essentially saying... is we will find a way to as as it's called demand destruction

insights.claim: The US is a net exporter of total petroleum products but is short crude specifically, importing ~6 million barrels and needing ~2-2.5 million barrels/day of heavy sour crude its refineries are built to process.
insights.speaker: David Salem
insights.so_what: The 'US is a net exporter' comfort is misleading; the quality mismatch leaves the US exposed to a crude import shock.
insights.quote: on crude no way we're short

insights.claim: The panel sketches a plausible late-summer/early-fall scenario where spot oil/diesel shortages force a 'mini-COVID' work-from-home episode or one-to-two-month recession, timed awkwardly close to midterms (which they think are already 'cooked').
insights.speaker: Sam / RPK
insights.so_what: A tail scenario worth hedging: a short, sharp oil-driven demand shock; energy/pipeline names (Kinder Morgan, FTI) are positioned as beneficiaries.
